title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu, and for acting as a knight-errant he had a reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was from Chu and had made a name for himself as a knight-errant who took up others' causes.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji ordered him to lead troops, and he repeatedly embarrassed the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu put him in command of troops, and time and again he cornered the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, Gaozu offered a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu; whoever dared shelter or conceal him would be guilty to the third degree of kin.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, the High Emperor posted a reward of a thousand pounds of gold for Ji Bu, with the threat that anyone who hid him would suffer the punishment of guilt extending to three degrees of kinship.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou clan of Puyang. The Zhou clan said, 'The Han are urgently seeking the general; their trail will soon reach my house. If you can heed me, I dare advance a plan;'
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with the Zhou family of Puyang. They told him, 'The court is hunting you relentlessly; their agents will trace you here before long. If you will listen to me, I have a plan to propose;'

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: if not, I wish first to cut my own throat.'
translations.idiomatic: If you will not, I would sooner cut my own throat first.'

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Bu assented.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Then they shaved Bu's head and put the cangue on him, clothed him in coarse cloth, placed him in a broad-willow cart, and together with several dozen of his household slaves went to the place of the Zhu family of Lu to sell him.
translations.idiomatic: They shaved his head, fitted him with the iron collar, dressed him in a beggar's russet, stowed him in a funeral wagon, and, with several dozen of his own household slaves in tow, drove him to the Zhu household in Lu to sell him off as a slave.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia in his heart knew he was Ji Bu, bought him, and placed him in a field house.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him at once as Ji Bu, purchased him, and lodged him on one of his farms.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Then he went to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Yiyin, Lord Teng, and urged, 'What crime has Ji Bu committed?'
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Yiyin, Lord Teng, and argued, 'What wrong has Ji Bu done?'

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each served their own lord in use; it was duty, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Each minister served his own master; that was nothing more than doing his duty.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: Could all the ministers of the Xiang clan be put to death?
translations.idiomatic: You cannot execute every minister who once served the Xiang house.

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire, yet for a private grudge he seeks one man—how does this show breadth!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm, yet he would hunt one man for a private slight—what kind of largeness of mind does that display?

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, with Ji Bu's worth, the Han seeking him so urgently—this is nothing but fleeing north to the Hu or south to Yue.
translations.idiomatic: A man of Ji Bu's caliber, hounded this fiercely, will flee north to the Xiongnu or south to Yue—that is all you will achieve.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: For to grudge a brave man and thereby strengthen an enemy state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped the tomb of King Ping of Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Spurning a formidable warrior only arms a rival power; it was for that reason Wu Zixu lashed the grave of King Ping of Chu.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why do you not at your ease speak to the sovereign on this?'
translations.idiomatic: Why not find a quiet moment and put this to the emperor yourself?'

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng in his heart knew Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant and suspected Bu was hidden with him, and so he promised.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Teng knew Zhu Jia for a famous knight-errant and guessed Ji Bu lay in his care, so he gave his word.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: When attending in a private moment, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had directed.
translations.idiomatic: When he next had the emperor's ear alone, he repeated Zhu Jia's argument point for point.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon pardoned Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then pardoned Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At that time the great men all praised Bu for being able to bend rigidity into softness, and Zhu Jia too for this became famous in the age.
translations.idiomatic: Men of rank admired Ji Bu for turning disaster into survival, and Zhu Jia's name rang through the realm for the same deed.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Bu was summoned for audience, thanked him, and was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was called to court, offered thanks, and appointed gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui, he was general of the gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaohui he served as general of the gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once sent a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress was angry and summoned the generals to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had once sent a letter that grossly insulted Empress Dowager Lü. Enraged, she called the generals in council.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: The senior general Fan Kuai said, 'Your servant wishes to obtain a hundred thousand men and march at will through the Xiongnu.'
translations.idiomatic: Senior general Fan Kuai declared, 'Give me a hundred thousand troops and I will sweep through the heart of the Xiongnu lands.'

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Kuai's words as right.
translations.idiomatic: The generals to a man curried favor with the empress dowager and agreed with Fan Kuai.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Bu said, 'Fan Kuai is fit to be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu said, 'Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: For with the High Emperor's troops of more than three hundred thousand, they were trapped at Pingcheng, and at that time Kuai was also among them.
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor took more than three hundred thousand men to Pingcheng and was surrounded there—and Fan Kuai was in that army.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how can Kuai with a hundred thousand men march at will through the Xiongnu—deceiving to the face!'
translations.idiomatic: How can the same man now promise to ride roughshod over the Xiongnu with a mere hundred thousand? That is bare-faced bravado.'

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, Qin on account of affairs with the Hu, Chen Sheng and others rose.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Qin court exhausted the realm on the northern frontier, and Chen Sheng and his like rose in rebellion.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now the wounds are not yet healed, and Kuai again flatters to the face, wishing to shake the empire.'
translations.idiomatic: The wounds of war have barely closed, and Fan Kuai would flatter you to your face and risk unsettling the whole realm again.'

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At that time those in the hall were all afraid; the empress dismissed court, and they never again deliberated on striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The courtiers in the hall were terrified. The empress dowager ended the session, and the plan to attack the Xiongnu was dropped.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Bu was governor of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was appointed governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, someone spoke of his worth, and he was summoned with the intention of making him imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaowen, someone praised his talent, and he was summoned to the capital with the idea of appointing him imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his courage, that he was unruly when drunk and hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others then spoke of his fierceness—that he was quarrelsome in his cups and difficult to work with.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was lodged in the guesthouse a month and dismissed without audience.
translations.idiomatic: He reached the capital and waited a full month in the official lodge before being sent away without an audience.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Bu advanced and said, 'Your servant awaits punishment at Hedong; the sovereign for no reason summoned me—this person must have had something by which to deceive the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward and said, 'I bore my office at Hedong in fear of fault; Your Majesty summoned me without cause—someone must have oversold me to you.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, received no assignment, and been dismissed to go—this person must have slandered your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Now I have come, been given no charge, and am dismissed—someone must have run me down behind my back.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: For the sovereign to summon your servant on one man's praise and send away your servant on one man's slander—your servant fears that men of judgment in the realm, hearing this, will thereby spy into the sovereign.'
translations.idiomatic: If a single voice of praise can bring me to court and a single whisper of blame can send me packing, I fear thoughtful men everywhere will read something into Your Majesty's judgment.'

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said, 'Hedong is my arm-and-thigh commandery; therefore I specially summoned you.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fell silent, then said with embarrassment, 'Hedong is one of the sinews of the realm; I called you for that reason alone.'

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Bu went to his post.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his duties in Hedong.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: The rhetorician Cao Qiusheng repeatedly angled for power and eyed money, served the great man Zhao Tan and others, and was on good terms with the elder Lord Dou.
translations.idiomatic: The persuader Cao Qiusheng traded on influence for gold, curried favor with men such as the powerful Zhao Tan, and was close to Lord Dou the elder.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: When Bu heard, he sent a letter remonstrating the elder Lord Dou, saying, 'I have heard Cao Qiusheng is not a man of stature; do not associate with him.'
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu heard of it and wrote to Lord Dou warning him, 'Cao Qiusheng is no gentleman; have nothing to do with him.'

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiusheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter and request Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng came home, he wanted a letter of introduction so he could call on Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: The elder Lord Dou said, 'General Ji is not pleased with you; do not go.'
translations.idiomatic: Lord Dou replied, 'General Ji dislikes you; you had better not go.'

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested a letter, and then went.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until he got the letter and set out.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He had someone send the letter ahead, and Bu was indeed greatly angry and waited for Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent a messenger ahead with the letter; Ji Bu flew into a rage and waited for him.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, he then bowed to Bu and said, 'The men of Chu have a proverb, "Better a hundred catties of gold than obtaining Ji Bu's promise"—how did you obtain this reputation between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng arrived, he bowed and said, 'The people of Chu say, "A hundred pounds of gold cannot match one promise from Ji Bu"—how did you earn such fame across Liang and Chu?'

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant and you are both men of Chu; were your servant to spread your name through the realm, would it not be fine?
translations.idiomatic: We are both sons of Chu; if I trumpeted your name to the world, would that not be splendid?'

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you keep your servant at such a distance!'
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so coldly!'

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Bu thereupon was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was delighted.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, kept him several months, treated him as a honored guest, and sent him off generously.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him inside, kept him for months as an honored guest, and sent him on his way with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Bu's name became more heard was that Cao Qiu spread it.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiusheng's praise that made Ji Bu's name resound even farther.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Bu's younger brother Ji Xin, in spirit covering Guanzhong, met people with respect and care, acted as knight-errant, over several thousand li, and gentlemen vied to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's younger brother Ji Xin dominated Guanzhong in sheer force of character, yet treated others with deference; as a knight-errant his fame ran a thousand miles, and fighting men competed for the honor of dying at his side.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man, fled to Wu, followed Yuan Si into hiding, served Yuan Si as an elder, and treated Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their like as younger brothers.
translations.idiomatic: He had once killed a man and fled to Wu, where he took refuge with Yuan Ang, serving him as an elder patron while treating Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their set as juniors.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was marshal of the center, and the commandant of the capital Zhi Du did not dare touch him.
translations.idiomatic: He served as marshal of the center; even Commandant Zhi Du of the capital did not dare cross him.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: Young men often secretly borrowed his name to act.
translations.idiomatic: Young blades habitually invoked his name without permission.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At that time, Ji Xin for courage and Bu for keeping promises were heard of in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Ji Xin was famed for valor and Ji Bu for keeping his word throughout Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Bu's younger brother by the same mother, Ding Gong, was a general for Xiang Yu and pursued and embarrassed Gaozu west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's maternal half-brother Ding Gong was a general under Xiang Yu who harried the High Emperor west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: With short weapons meeting, the King of Han was in straits, looked back and said to Ding Gong, 'Can two worthies hem each other in!'
translations.idiomatic: In close combat the King of Han was hard pressed, turned to Ding Gong, and cried, 'Two good men do not corner each other!'

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Ding Gong led his troops and withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Gong called off his troops and drew back.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When King Xiang was destroyed, Ding Gong requested audience with Gaozu; he took Ding Gong through the army as an example, saying, 'Ding Gong was not loyal as a minister to King Xiang, and was one who made King Xiang lose the realm.'
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Ding Gong presented himself to the High Emperor, who paraded him before the host and declared, 'Ding Gong served King Xiang without loyalty; he is the sort of man who cost his master the empire.'

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon he beheaded him, saying, 'Let those afterward who are ministers to others not imitate Ding Gong!'
translations.idiomatic: He had him beheaded, adding, 'Let no future minister take Ding Gong as his model!'

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was a native of Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he once roamed with Bu; in poverty they sold labor in Qi and worked as a tavern bailiff.
translations.idiomatic: While Peng Yue was still a commoner, he and Luan Bu had traveled together; destitute, they hired out as laborers in Qi and Luan Bu tended tables in an inn.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: Several years later they parted, and Bu was kidnapped by someone and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Years later they went separate ways, and Luan Bu was seized and sold into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: For his master's household he avenged a wrong; the Yan general Zang Tu raised him and made him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged his master's family; the Yan general Zang Tu singled him out and appointed him commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became King of Yan, Bu was a general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became king of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, the Han struck Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu rose in rebellion, the Han forces attacked Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King Liang Peng Yue heard of it and spoke to the sovereign, asking to ransom Bu and make him a grandee of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang petitioned the throne to ransom him and appoint him a grandee of Liang.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: He was sent on mission to Qi; before he returned, the Han summoned Peng Yue and charged him with plotting rebellion, exterminated him to the third degree of kin, displayed the head at Luoyang, and issued an edict that whoever collected or viewed him would be arrested at once.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was on a mission to Qi and had not returned when the court summoned Peng Yue, condemned him for treason, wiped out three degrees of his kin, exposed his head at Luoyang, and decreed that anyone who dared mourn or view the remains would be seized.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported his business beneath Peng Yue's head, sacrificed, and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu came back, made his report beneath Peng Yue's severed head, offered sacrifice, and wept.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: The clerks arrested him and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Officials seized him and informed the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled him, saying, 'Did you plot rebellion with Peng Yue?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had Luan Bu dragged in and shouted, 'Did you conspire with Peng Yue?'

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade people not to collect him; you alone sacrificed and wept for him—with rebellion it is clear.'
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to mourn him; you alone held rites and wept—that is open rebellion.'

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hurry and boil him.'
translations.idiomatic: Boil him at once.'

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as he was being lifted toward the cauldron, he looked back and said, 'I wish one word and then to die.'
translations.idiomatic: As they hauled him toward the boiling cauldron, he turned and said, 'Let me speak one sentence before I die.'

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, 'What words?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, 'What is it?'

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said, 'When the sovereign was trapped at Pengcheng, defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason King Xiang could not press west was only that King Peng held Liang territory and allied with Han to distress Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said, 'When you were pinned down at Pengcheng and driven back between Xingyang and Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu could not push west was that King Peng held the Liang lands in alliance with Han and tormented Chu from the rear.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At that time, had King Peng turned his glance once—with Chu then Han would have broken, with Han then Chu would have broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue's loyalty would have shattered Han if he joined Chu, or shattered Chu if he stayed with Han.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the meeting at Gaixia, without King Peng, the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: At Gaixia, without Peng Yue, the house of Xiang would not have fallen.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: When the realm was settled, King Peng received the tallies and investiture, and also wished to pass them on for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: Peace won, King Peng received his bronze tally and fief, intending them to pass down for generations.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now the Han once summoned troops from Liang; King Peng was ill and did not go, and they suspected him as rebelling.
translations.idiomatic: Now a single call-up from Liang found him too ill to march, and you branded him a traitor.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: The signs of rebellion not yet seen, to execute him on petty grounds—your servant fears that meritorious ministers will each fear for himself.
translations.idiomatic: No act of rebellion had appeared; to kill him on trifling suspicion will make every accomplished minister wonder who is next.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now King Peng is dead; your servant to live is not as good as to die; I request to go to the boiling.'
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue is dead; for me to live would be worse than death. Send me to the cauldron.'

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him and appointed him commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, he was chancellor of Yan, reaching to general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaowen he rose from chancellor of Yan to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared, saying, 'In straits and unable to endure humiliation to oneself is not to be human;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu used to say, 'To be poor and yet refuse to demean yourself is less than human;'

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: wealth and honor and unable to satisfy one's will is not to be worthy.'
translations.idiomatic: To be rich and honored and yet unable to satisfy your desires is less than worthy.'

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Thereupon if he had received kindness, he repaid it generously;
translations.idiomatic: Those who had done him a kindness he repaid lavishly;

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: if he had a grudge, he was sure to destroy them by law.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who had earned his enmity he destroyed by legal process.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, for merit he was enfeoffed as marquis of Yu and again was chancellor of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: During the revolt of Wu and Chu he earned the title Marquis of Yu and returned as chancellor of Yan.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi they all established shrines for him, styled the shrines of Lord Luan.
translations.idiomatic: From Yan to Qi the people set up shrines to him, calling them the altars of Lord Luan.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: Bu died; his son Ben succeeded the marquisate; in the time of Emperor Xiaowu he was convicted because as grand master of ceremonies the sacrificial victims did not meet regulations, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu died and his son Luan Ben inherited the marquisate; under Emperor Wu he lost the fief for letting sacrificial animals fall short of ritual standards while serving as grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Xingcheng in Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu came from Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Tian clan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears were the Tian lineage of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu loved the sword and studied Huang-Lao techniques with Lord Yue Ju.
translations.idiomatic: He loved fencing and studied Huang-Lao doctrine under Yue Ju.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: As a man he was incorrupt and straight, and delighted in knight-errantry.
translations.idiomatic: He was honest, upright, and drawn to the knight-errant's code.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He roamed among the great men; men of Zhao recommended him to the Zhao minister Zhao Wu, who spoke of him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, and he was made gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved in elite circles until the people of Zhao recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who mentioned him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, who appointed him gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: Several years the king deemed him worthy, but before he was promoted.
translations.idiomatic: For several years the king admired him but had not yet promoted him.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; the affair was discovered, and the Han issued an edict to arrest the King of Zhao and the ministers who had rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and their faction plotted to kill the emperor; the plot was uncovered, and Han issued warrants for King Zhang Ao of Zhao and every minister implicated in treason.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: In Zhao whoever dared follow the king would be guilty to the third degree of kin.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhao anyone who dared stand by the king would share guilt to the third degree of kinship.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others in crimson garb shaved their own heads and put on the cangue, following the king to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen companions donned convict russet, shaved their own heads, and clamped on the iron collar to escort their king to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: King Ao of Zhao's case was cleared; he was able to leave, was removed as king and made Marquis of Xuanping, and then advanced word recommending Shu and the ten men.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Ao's name was cleared, he left prison, was stripped of his kingship and enfeoffed as Marquis of Xuanping, and then recommended Tian Shu and his nine companions to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned them for audience and conversed with them; no minister of the Han court could surpass them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received them and spoke with each in turn; not one minister at court could match their poise.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased and appointed them all governors or chancellors to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased with them all, he named every man either a commandery governor or a feudal chancellor.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was governor of Hanzhong for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for well over a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had just taken the throne, he summoned Shu and asked, 'Do you know who are the most worthy elders in the realm?'
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Xiaowen's reign he called Tian Shu in and asked, 'Who in the empire commands respect as a true elder statesman?'

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied, 'Your servant how could be sufficient to know that!'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, 'How could a man like me presume to judge such things?'

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, 'You are an elder; you ought to know.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, 'You yourself are such a man; you must have an opinion.'

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, 'The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is such a man.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, 'The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is the one I would name.'

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At that time Meng Shu sat convicted because the barbarians had entered Yunzhong in great force and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been stripped of office for letting a large Xiongnu raid penetrate Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said, 'The late emperor established Meng Shu in Yunzhong for more than ten years; the barbarians once entered in force; Meng Shu could not hold firm; for no reason soldiers died in battle several hundred men—do worthies indeed kill people?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor objected: 'The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for years; the Xiongnu broke in once in strength, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of men died without cause. Is that what you call the conduct of a humane elder?'

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: 'Do men of virtue go about killing people?'
translations.idiomatic: Do worthy men make a habit of sending men to their deaths?'

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, 'When Guan Gao and the others plotted rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty to the third degree of kin; yet Meng Shu shaved his own head and put on the cangue to follow King Zhang, ready to die with him—how could he have known he would become governor of Yunzhong!'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and replied, 'When Guan Gao conspired treason, the court decreed that anyone in Zhao who stayed with King Zhang Ao would die with three degrees of kin—yet Meng Shu mutilated himself like a convict and followed his lord into captivity, prepared to die. He could not then have dreamed he would one day govern Yunzhong.'

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; the soldiers were worn out, while the Xiongnu Modu had newly subdued the northern tribes and came as border raiders. Meng Shu knew the troops were exhausted and could not bear to order them out; the men vied to mount the walls and die against the foe like sons for a father—therefore several hundred died. How could Meng Shu have driven them!'
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu were locked in stalemate and the army was spent; Modu had just cowed the northern peoples and turned south to harry the frontier. Meng Shu saw how broken his men were and could not bring himself to drive them out; the soldiers themselves rushed to the walls and died like sons defending a father—hence the heavy losses. Meng Shu did not hound them to that fate.'

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This is the reason Meng Shu is a worthy elder.'
translations.idiomatic: That is precisely what makes Meng Shu a true elder.'

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said, 'How worthy is Meng Shu!'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, 'Meng Shu is a remarkable man!'

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: He again summoned him and made him governor of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He recalled Meng Shu and restored him as governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: Several years later Shu lost office because of a legal offense.
translations.idiomatic: Some years afterward Tian Shu lost his post over a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: Prince Xiao of Liang had someone kill the Han councilor Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang, and he fully obtained the facts.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Xiao of Liang had the Han adviser Yuan Ang murdered, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to probe the kingdom of Liang, and he extracted the whole truth.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning, he reported; the sovereign said, 'Did Liang have it?'
translations.idiomatic: On his return the emperor asked him bluntly, 'Did Liang do it?'

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied, 'There is.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, 'Yes.'

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: 'Where is the affair?'
translations.idiomatic: 'What proof do you have?'

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said, 'Your Majesty had best not press the matter of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, 'Your Majesty should not pursue this question about Liang.'

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: Now if the Prince of Liang is not executed, this is casting aside the law of Han;
translations.idiomatic: If the Prince of Liang is not punished, you cast aside the law of Han.

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he is executed, the empress dowager will not find her food sweet nor her couch easy—this care falls on Your Majesty.'
translations.idiomatic: If you put him to death, the empress dowager will neither eat nor sleep at ease, and that sorrow will fall on Your Majesty alone.'

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his tact and appointed him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: When the chancellor first took office, commoners who on their own spoke out that the king had taken their goods numbered more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he took office, more than a hundred people came forward on their own, accusing the king of seizing their property.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: Shu took their ringleaders, two thousand men, and flogged them, angrily saying, 'Is the king not your lord? How dare you speak against your lord on your own!'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu had the ringleaders—some two thousand men—rounded up and flogged, shouting, 'The king is your sovereign; how dare you denounce him in open court!'

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: 'How dare you on your own speak against the lord!'
translations.idiomatic: Who gave you leave to accuse your master?'

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: The King of Lu heard, was greatly ashamed, opened the central treasury's cash, and had the chancellor compensate them.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu was mortified, opened the palace treasury, and told the chancellor to pay the claimants.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said, 'Let the king himself send men to make restitution; if not, it is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good.'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu refused: 'Send your own stewards to make amends; otherwise it will look as though you committed the wrong and I bought cheap virtue.'

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The King of Lu loved hunting; the chancellor often followed him into the park, and the king always had the chancellor rest and go to the lodge.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; Tian Shu always rode with him into the hunting park, and the king would dismiss him to the guesthouse to rest.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park and to the end did not rest, saying, 'Our king is exposed to wind and sun—why alone should I take shelter?'
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu instead sat in the open outside the preserve and refused to leave, saying, 'If my king endures the weather, how can I hide indoors?'

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: For this reason the king for the most part did not go out roaming.
translations.idiomatic: Shamed into it, the king gave up most of his long hunting trips.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: Several years later he died in office; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for sacrifice; the younger son Ren would not accept, saying, 'By righteousness I do not harm the prior man's name.'
translations.idiomatic: He died in office a few years later. Lu offered a hundred pounds of gold for his rites, but his youngest son Tian Ren declined, saying, 'Right feeling forbids me to trade on my father's reputation.'

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren for strength and courage was a retainer of General Wei and repeatedly followed in striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Ren, bold and hardy, served as a household retainer to General Wei Qing and rode with him on many campaigns against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: General Wei advanced word recommending Ren as gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand shi and chief clerk to the chancellor, then lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing recommended him for gentleman of the palace; he rose to two thousand shi and chief clerk under the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Later he was sent to censor the Three He region; returning, his memorial pleased the sovereign, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was sent as imperial censor to the Three He circuit; his memorial on returning pleased the emperor, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant of the capital region.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After more than a month he was promoted to imperial investigator.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a month later he was promoted to imperial investigator.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: Several years later, when Crown Prince Li raised troops, Ren's section closed the city gates so that the crown prince could flee; he sat convicted for letting the rebel go and his clan was exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, when Heir Apparent Liu Ju rose in arms, Tian Ren, commanding his detachment, had the city gates shut in a manner that allowed the prince to slip away; he was convicted of releasing a traitor and his whole clan was put to death.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: With Xiang Yu's fierce temper, yet Ji Bu by courage displayed his name in Chu; personally he trod battle lines and tore enemy banners many times—he may be called a stalwart.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: For all of Xiang Yu's towering rage, Ji Bu won fame in Chu through sheer nerve, time and again charging the line and snatching enemy standards—a true fighting man.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: Coming to being trapped and humiliated as a slave, clinging to life without changing—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet when ruin drove him to the disgrace of slavery, he endured and did not break—why was that?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: They trusted their own talents; receiving insult without shame, they wished to apply what still lay unused in them; therefore in the end they became famous generals of Han.
translations.idiomatic: They trusted their own ability; they could bear shame because they still had more to give the world, and so they finished as celebrated generals of Han.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: Worthy men indeed take their deaths seriously.
translations.idiomatic: Men of true worth do not throw their lives away cheaply.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: As for maids, concubines, and mean persons, stirred to indignation and killing themselves—they cannot be called brave; their scheming is utterly shallow.
translations.idiomatic: Maids, concubines, and common folk who take offense and fall on their blades are not brave; their outlook is hopelessly mean and narrow.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao; they went to death as if returning home—they truly knew their place; though the steadfast heroes of antiquity, what could surpass them!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue at the risk of his life; Tian Shu shackled himself to Zhang Ao and marched toward execution as calmly as a man going home. They knew exactly where they stood; the steadfast worthies of old could scarcely have outdone them.
